title: Pahārādasutta
claim: nāgas dwell in the great ocean as one of the classes of mighty beings (timi, asuras, nāgas, gandhabbas)

title: Uposathasutta
claim: nāgas are among the mighty beings dwelling in the great ocean

title: 3. Mahāparinibbānasuttaṃ
claim: a nāga-king of Rāmagāma venerates a relic-portion of the Buddha; nāgas as relic-keepers honoured beside deva/nāga/nara kings

title: 7. Mahāsamayasuttaṃ
claim: nāgas (Yamuna, Dhataraṭṭha clans; Erāvaṇa the great nāga) attend the great assembly; the Buddha grants them safety from the supaṇṇas who seize nāga-kings

title: 8. Sakkapañhasuttaṃ
claim: nāgas listed among devas, manussas, asuras, gandhabbas and other classes (puthukāyā) bound by fetters; nāga as a class of being

title: 7. Lakkhaṇasuttaṃ
claim: gandhabbas and nāgas (gandhabbanāgā), winged ones and quadrupeds attend the great being; nāgas as a class of attendant beings

title: 9. Āṭānāṭiyasuttaṃ
claim: nāgas have their own social orders — nāgo/nāgī/nāgapotaka/nāgamahāmatto/nāgapārisajja/nāgapacāra (lord, female, young, minister, councillor); parallel to gandhabba and kumbhaṇḍa hierarchies who may harass monks

title: 3. Diṭṭhisaṃyuttaṃ
claim: Makkhali's cosmology enumerates 4,900 nāga-abodes (ekūnapaññāsa nāgavāsasate) among the fixed categories of beings/rebirths

title: 8. Nāgasaṃyuttaṃ
claim: there are four nāga-wombs: egg-born, womb-born, moisture-born, spontaneously-born nāgas (the four nāgayoni)

title: 9. Supaṇṇasaṃyuttaṃ
claim: supaṇṇas of each birth-mode carry off nāgas; egg-born supaṇṇas seize only egg-born nāgas, etc.; nāgas as the prey of supaṇṇas

title: 1. Maggasaṃyuttaṃ
claim: nāgas grow their bodies and gather strength depending on the Himalaya, then descend to the pools and to the great ocean; their habitat and growth

title: 2. Mahāvaggo
claim: nagas (with asuras and gandhabbas) dwell as mighty beings in the great ocean

title: 8. Nidhikaṇḍasuttaṃ
claim: nagas can spirit away/remove buried treasure (as yakkhas carry it off)

title: 5. Soṇavaggo (mahāvagga (aṭṭhakathāya sameti))
claim: nagas (with asuras, gandhabbas) dwell as great beings in the ocean

title: 4. Khattiyāvaggo
claim: devas, kinnaras and nagas (former retinue) will attain awakening in a future time

title: 21. Vipassībuddhavaṃso
claim: the Bodhisatta was once a mighty naga-king (Atula) with millions of nagas, who honored Buddha Vipassi

title: 6. Sumanabuddhavaṃso
claim: the Bodhisatta was a mighty naga-king Atula who came from the naga-abode (nagabhavana) with his kin to honor Buddha Sumana

title: 2. Hatthināgavaggo
claim: the vagga collects the Bodhisatta's lives as the naga-kings Campeyya and Sankhapala (with Bhuridatta), who kept their virtue as nagas

title: 12. Dvādasakanipāto
claim: a naga has a spread hood (phana) and coils (bhoga) and caught a falling prince with its coils

title: 15. Vīsatinipāto
claim: the naga-clan (with its maidens and dwellings) lives under the supannas' predatory enmity; the king secures supanna non-aggression toward the nagas

title: 2. Dukanipāto
claim: a naga, by its power, took the form of a ship to carry a faithful layman safely across the sea

title: 17. Cattālīsanipāto
claim: a mighty naga-king of divine power keeps austerities/uposatha longing for human rebirth, holding the human world superior (for the path)

title: 22. Mahānipāto
claim: Bhuridatta Jataka: the Bodhisatta as a naga-king, with naga-maidens (Irandhati), the naga-clan and naga-realm, and naga marriage

title: 2. Saṅghādisesakaṇḍaṃ
claim: Maṇikaṇṭha the nāga-king crosses the Ganges, coils a sage sevenfold and rears a great hood over him

title: 1. Mahākhandhako
claim: the fierce fire-room nāgarāja is iddhimā, āsiviso, ghoraviso (potent, venomous serpent); the Buddha subdues it

title: 6. Bhesajjakkhandhako
claim: a nāga dwelling at the Mandākinī lotus-pond gives Moggallāna lotus-stalks and commands another nāga

title: 9. Pātimokkhaṭṭhapanakkhandhakaṃ
claim: the great ocean is the abode of great beings — timi, asurā, nāgā, gandhabbā — with bodies up to many leagues

title: Mahāparinibbānasutta
claim: a nāga-king venerates a relic-share at Rāmagāma and one of the Buddha's tooth-relics; nāga-kings hold/honor relics

title: Mahāsamayasutta
claim: Mahāsamaya: nāgas (Nāgasā, Vesālā, Tacchakā, Kambala-Assatara, Yāmunā, Dhataraṭṭhā, Erāvaṇa) come to the assembly; supaṇṇas threaten them and the Buddha makes peace; nāgas take refuge

title: Āṭānāṭiyasutta
claim: Āṭānāṭiya: nāgo/nāgī/nāgapotako/nāgamahāmatto/nāgapārisajja — nāgas have a society (males, females, young, ministers, courtiers) parallel to gandhabbas/kumbhaṇḍas

title: 10. Sīlānisaṁsajātaka
claim: a nāga ferries a faithful lay-devotee across by boat (Nāgo nāvāya vaṇṇena saddhaṃ vahatupāsakaṃ) — fruit of faith/virtue

title: 3. Maṇikaṇṭhajātaka
claim: a nāga owns a wish-granting gem (maṇi); when a brahmin begs it repeatedly the nāga withdraws and never reappears

title: 10. Sīlavīmaṁsakajātaka
claim: even a venomous nāga (ghoraviso nāgo) is not killed because it is virtuous (sīlavā) — virtue protects

title: 10. Sīlavīmaṁsajātaka
claim: a venomous nāga (ghoraviso nāgo), being virtuous, is not slain — virtue spares it

title: 7. Laṭukikajātaka
claim: a nāga-king is destroyed by weak creatures (crow, frog, fly) banding together — even a mighty nāga falls to enmity

title: 10. Bakajātaka
claim: a malicious nāga seizes a boat in the Ganges current and a being rescues the men from it

title: 3. Catuposathiyajātaka
claim: the nāga-king (with garuḷa/venateyya, gandhabba-king, the Kuru king) declares his virtue is khanti (patience) — nāga as one of four exemplary kings

title: 9. Mahāpadumajātaka
claim: a hooded nāga (nāgo jātaphaṇo), mighty, caught the falling prince in its coils and saved him in a mountain abyss

title: 10. Mahāvāṇijajātaka
claim: nāgas emerge fully armed (sannaddhā paṇḍavīsati) with archers to attack tree-fellers — a nāga host guarding/avenging

title: 10. Campeyyajātaka
claim: Campeyya the nāga-king keeps uposatha as a serpent (uraga), is captured by a snake-charmer, freed, and invites the king to his nāga palace; bodhisatta-as-nāga

title: 8. Paṇḍaranāgarājajātaka
claim: the nāga-king Paṇḍaraka is seized by the supaṇṇa once his secret is betrayed (Nāgaṃ yathā paṇḍarakaṃ supaṇṇo)

title: 4. Saṅkhapālajātaka
claim: Saṅkhapāla the nāga-king keeps uposatha enduring torment by hunters, longing to be reborn human (manussayoniṃ abhipatthayāno); bodhisatta-as-nāga

title: 1 Cūḷahaṁsajātaka

title: 6. Bhūridattajātaka
claim: Bhūridatta the nāga: a snake-charmer threatens 'alampāno hi nāgena' and boasts of mastering venomless/venomous nāgas; the nāga is the bodhisatta serpent

title: Vidhurajātaka
claim: Vidhura: the nāga realm with nāga-king/queen (nāgarājā, nāgi), nāga-maidens (nāgakaññā), nāga-kingdom (nāgakula); a nāgi practising non-malice attains the deva-world

title: 6. Sīlavīmaṁsakajātaka
claim: a venomous nāga (ghoraviso nāgo), being virtuous, is not killed — virtue protects it

title: Nidhikaṇḍasutta
claim: Nidhikaṇḍa: nāgas can spirit away a buried treasure (Nāgā vā apanāmenti), as yakkhas carry it off

title: Bālapaṇḍitasutta
claim: [auto strong-serpent; facet TBD]

title: Vammikasutta
claim: Vammika riddle: digging the anthill one finds a nāga; 'leave the nāga, do not strike it, pay homage' — the nāga = the arahant (allegory), literal referent a serpent in the mound

title: Sandakasutta
claim: [auto strong-serpent; facet TBD]

title: 2. Sāsanapaṭṭhānadutiyabhūmi
claim: The Buddha tames/teaches devas, nāgas and yakkhas in their own dialects via Dhamma

title: 51. Surāpānasikkhāpada
claim: A nāga in the fire-room smokes and blazes; its fire is mastered only by the fire-element

title: 6. Kuṭikārasikkhāpada
claim: The nāga-king Maṇikaṇṭha coils a sage sevenfold and rears a great hood

title: 1. Mahākhandhaka
claim: A fierce nāga-king of the fire-room is potent, terribly venomous; the Buddha subdues its fire

title: Suddhikasutta
claim: There are four nāga births: egg-born, womb-born, moisture-born, spontaneously-born

title: Catutthasutasutta
claim: Ambivalent (dual) bodily/verbal/mental conduct + craving leads to rebirth among spontaneously-born nāgas

title: Aṇḍajadānūpakārasuttadasaka
claim: Dual conduct plus specific giving leads to rebirth among egg-born nāgas (dīghāyu, vaṇṇavanto)

title: Paṇītatarasutta
claim: Among the four nāga births each later mode is superior to the egg-born

title: Jalābujādidānūpakārasuttattiṁsaka
claim: Dual conduct plus specific giving leads to rebirth among womb/moisture/spontaneously-born nāgas

title: Uposathasutta
claim: Egg-born nāgas keep the uposatha and so release the body, recalling their former dual conduct

title: Dutiyauposathasutta
claim: Womb-born nāgas keep the uposatha and release the body

title: Tatiyauposathasutta
claim: Moisture-born nāgas keep the uposatha and release the body

title: Catutthauposathasutta
claim: Spontaneously-born nāgas keep the uposatha, resolving on good conduct to escape the nāga state

title: Sutasutta
claim: Dual conduct, with hearing that egg-born nāgas are long-lived/handsome, leads to rebirth among them

title: Dutiyasutasutta
claim: Dual conduct leads to rebirth among womb-born nāgas

title: Tatiyasutasutta
claim: Dual conduct leads to rebirth among moisture-born nāgas

title: Harantisutta
claim: Supaṇṇas seize nāgas only of their own or lower birth-class (egg/womb/moisture/spontaneous)

title: Bakabrahmasutta
claim: A cruel nāga in the Ganges seizes a boat, craving human prey; the bodhisatta frees it

title: Dhammikasutta
claim: [auto strong-serpent; facet TBD]

title: Mahāmoggallānattheraapadāna

title: Ajitattheraapadāna
claim: nāgas and garuḷas come to the Buddha desiring to hear Dhamma

title: Sabbaphaladāyakattheraapadāna
claim: long-lived nāgas, possessed of iddhi and great renown, come under the donor's sway

title: Uppalavaṇṇātherīapadāna
claim: a nāga-king hosts and feeds the Buddha in the nāga realm and a nāga-maiden makes the anumodanā

title: Mucalindasutta

title: Uposathasutta
claim: the great ocean is the dwelling of mighty beings including nāgas, with bodies up to hundreds of yojanas
